text: The laparoscopic setup includes an umbilical port as the first working port, a suprapubic port as the second working port, and a left iliac fossa port for the camera. The triangulation provides optimal access to the appendix, with visible pus in the pouch of Douglas (POD).

The surgeon observes peristalsis of the ureter and ensures the small bowel is moved away from the dissection field in the right iliac fossa. The patient is positioned head low and right lateral up to facilitate identification of the appendix. Purulent fluid in the right iliac fossa is suctioned, and the cecum's mobility is assessed. The appendix, located below the cecum, is identified and manipulated to expose the mesoappendix. The surgeon uses monopolar cautery to dissect between the appendix and mesoappendix, ensuring the appendix is isolated for easy retrieval through the camera or working port. Care is taken to avoid conducting energy to surrounding structures.

The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery to dissect the mesoappendix from the appendix, applying traction and counter-traction to precisely separate the structures. The appendix is twisted to clearly identify the mesoappendix, and the dissection is performed close to the appendix to avoid large branches of the appendicular artery, minimizing bleeding. The elongated appendix is visible, with the base identified where the three tinea converge, and the cecum is seen beyond the base.

The surgeon manipulates the bulky mesoappendix to achieve a precise angle for dissection, using bipolar cautery to minimize thermal spread near the small bowel and cecum. After securing hemostasis, monopolar cautery is used to open tissue planes, with traction pulling the appendix away from the cecum. Thin fibrous strands are visible, and the appendicular artery's course is appreciated as dissection progresses.

The surgeon uses a short burst of cautery with the Maryland dissector to carefully separate strands of tissue from the appendix, ensuring the dissection remains close to the appendix to maintain a thin specimen. A small vessel from the appendicular artery is coagulated using bipolar cautery due to its proximity to the cecum, preventing potential bleeding from retracted vessels. The dissection progresses towards the base of the appendix, creating a clear window to the area.

The surgeon cuts the coagulated vessel, causing the mesoappendix to retract and reveal the widening structure of the cecum. The base of the appendix is dissected, and an endo loop with chromic catgut absorbable suture is applied. Two loops are placed proximally and one distally to prevent spillage from the appendix lumen. The surgeon milks the appendix lumen to dislodge any fecolith into the distal portion before applying the third loop.

The surgeon performs a bowel walk to rule out Meckel's diverticulum, then cuts the appendicular specimen, revealing inflammatory fluid but no fecolith. The appendicular stump is cauterized using bipolar electrocautery. The appendix is retrieved through the ten mm camera port, which is then closed with an absorbable suture. Local anesthesia is infiltrated using a laparoscopic-guided TAP block for post-operative analgesia.
